prompt: Search X for 2026 reactions to AI AGENT SECURITY incidents/breaches (e.g., Mercor April 2026, prompt-injection, over-permissioned agents, MCP risks). What are founders/security people saying, and does it create buying urgency for agent-security proof? Quote posts + handles. Cite.
content:
**Mercor April 2026 breach (supply-chain attack via LiteLLM dependency, attributed to Lapsus$/TeamPCP) triggered significant discussion on X, highlighting AI agent/vendor supply-chain risks, biometric/contractor data exposure (up to 4TB including voice samples, Slack, source code, and client data from OpenAI/Anthropic/Meta), class-action lawsuits, and downstream impacts like Meta pausing partnerships.**[[1]](https://x.com/AlvieriD/status/2038779690295378004)[[2]](https://x.com/smallbbear/status/2049764360952987775)

Security-focused accounts and founders emphasized third-party/vendor risk, open-source dependency vetting, and contractor ecosystems as new attack surfaces. Broader 2026 conversations (prompt injection, over-permissioned agents, MCP/Model Context Protocol risks like optional auth, command injection, tool poisoning, and exposed servers) stress that these are access/governance problems—not purely model issues—and call for least-privilege, scoped/ephemeral credentials, input scanning, isolation, and treating agents as governed IAM identities.[[3]](https://x.com/dagomint/status/2061313628649709589)[[4]](https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2061388039188799699)

These incidents appear to be amplifying urgency for agent-security tooling, governance frameworks, red-teaming, and proof-of-security capabilities, as posts note governance gaps (e.g., only ~21% of enterprises with matching models despite high agent adoption) and position proactive measures as essential before (or after) incidents.[[5]](https://x.com/stormrae_ai/status/2046281126814343458)

### Key Quoted Posts from Founders/Security People (2026)
- **@Cybersolbv** (focuses on EU third-party risk, AI obligations, NIS2): “Mercor breach exposes critical supply chain exposure: AI vendor compromised via LiteLLM dependency, affecting downstream customers. Governance gap: vendor notification timelines, contractual liability allocation, and NIS2 third-party risk frameworks now under scrutiny.” (Apr 30, 2026)[[6]](https://x.com/Cybersolbv/status/2049878946515214560)  
  Follow-up: “LiteLLM supply chain compromise exposes thousands of downstream users to vendor risk cascade. Mercor breach signals critical gap: open-source dependency vetting absent from most vendor onboarding frameworks.”[[7]](https://x.com/Cybersolbv/status/2049837422750343407)

- **@lior_pozin** (bootstrapped founder, ex-AutoDS): “4TB of voice samples stolen from 40,000 AI contractors at Mercor. This is the contractor economy’s first major data breach. Your data perimeter ends where your contractors’ laptops begin.” (Apr 28, 2026)[[8]](https://x.com/lior_pozin/status/2049247738466820598)

- **@dagomint** (building security infrastructure for AI agents): “The 2026 AI agent threat list, short version: → Prompt injection via web content the agent reads → Command injection in MCP server configs → Credential exposure when agents touch wallets/keys → Supply chain: poisoned packages the agent installs. None of these are model problems. All of them are access problems. The fixes are known: scan untrusted input, isolate execution, enforce least privilege. The hard part is doing it before the incident, not after.” (Jun 1, 2026)[[3]](https://x.com/dagomint/status/2061313628649709589)

- **@DFIR_Radar** (DFIR/intel account): Details MCP server scale (>13k integrations, high vuln rates to command injection/SSRF/tool poisoning), indirect prompt injection, agent-to-agent impersonation, and forensic challenges. Recommends: “Treat AI agents as principals in IAM - inventory, scope, monitor, and review on same cadence as privileged service accounts.” (Jun 1, 2026)[[4]](https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2061388039188799699)

- **@svpino** (AI/ML engineering educator): On MCP enabling agent tools: “But nobody is talking about how to secure these servers. ... Agents are fundamentally different from humans: • They should be able to act autonomously • ... Their credentials should be short-lived • Their credentials should be scoped as tightly as possible. Traditional IAM systems don’t work for this.” (Mar 3, 2026)[[9]](https://x.com/svpino/status/2028865502836408611)

- **@Basemail_ai**: “MCP — the protocol connecting all AI agents — just got its first NSA warning. Authorization is OPTIONAL at protocol level. 12,520 exposed servers. 67 CVEs across 40K repos. 81% attack success rate.” (Jun 5, 2026)[[10]](https://x.com/Basemail_ai/status/2062830366755533097)

- **@stormrae_ai** (AI security focus): “Roughly 96% of enterprises are already running AI agents. Only 21% have a governance model to match. This is the most dangerous gap in tech right now. ... OWASP’s Q2 2026 landscape report names the top threats: prompt injection, agent privilege escalation, data poisoning... These aren’t theoretical. They’re happening in production. ... Red-teaming shouldn’t be a line item. It should be infrastructure.” (Apr 20, 2026)[[5]](https://x.com/stormrae_ai/status/2046281126814343458)

- **@AntonMartyniuk** (architect/MVP): MCP security note on “Controlled access” being only as strong as the authorization layer; warns of prompt injection via tool results and need for allow-lists, input validation, and human approval/audit for production modifications. (Jun 1, 2026)[[11]](https://x.com/AntonMartyniuk/status/2061336008512180431)

Additional context from the results includes litigation/supply-chain fallout for Mercor clients and calls for dependency vetting, ephemeral tokens, task-scoped tools (not 30+ per agent), and behavioral controls over “trusted” agents.[[12]](https://x.com/rangeva/status/2049896619076698241)[[13]](https://x.com/byumut/status/2063607743555006784)

Overall, the tone from security practitioners and founders is one of heightened awareness and practical urgency—incidents like Mercor are cited as proof that existing gaps in permissions, supply chain, and MCP implementations demand immediate investment in agent-specific security controls and verifiable governance.
